cycles.name: FOUNDATION FORGE
focuses: Build foundational movement quality and neuromuscular efficiency across all major patterns.
Establish aerobic base and running consistency toward half marathon preparation.

paragraphs: This is where everything starts — and it matters more than any other phase. Foundation Forge is not about pushing limits, it’s about building the platform every future result will stand on.

Your nervous system is learning to recruit muscle efficiently under structured load. Your joints, tendons, and connective tissue are adapting to movement patterns they haven’t been consistently exposed to before. At the same time, your aerobic engine is being developed to support longer, more sustainable efforts.
The temptation will be to go heavier, faster, or harder than prescribed. Resist it. The tempo, the RPE caps, and the movement quality are the programme — not just the exercises themselves.
outcomes: Noticeably improved strength in squat and hinge patterns
Greater core stability and control during running
Ability to run continuously for 30+ minutes without stopping

cycles.name: STRENGTH ENGINE
focuses: Increase relative strength through progressive overload on compound movements.
Extend run duration and introduce structured tempo running intervals.

teaser.subtitle: You’ve built the foundation. Now we load it.
paragraphs: Cycle 2 is where the body begins to genuinely change — the movement patterns you’ve developed now become vehicles for real strength development through progressive overload.
Running evolves from general cardio into structured training, with longer durations and the introduction of tempo-based intervals to build performance.
The work gets heavier, the sessions become more targeted, and the gap between your current level and your potential starts to close rapidly.
Earn it in these five weeks. It’s waiting.

cycles.name: METABOLIC SHIFT
focuses: Drive body recomposition through higher training density and elevated metabolic demand.
Develop single-leg and running-specific strength for race durability.

cycles.name: RACE READY
focuses: Peak running volume and muscular endurance for half marathon completion.
Maintain lean muscle and reduce session fatigue through smart load management.

cycles.name: CONSOLIDATE & ELEVATE
focuses: Integrate all physical qualities into a sustainable, high-performance lifestyle structure.
Post-race recovery, reflection, and transition into long-term athletic identity.

days.focusTag: Full Body Strength A — Compound Foundation
days.completionMessage: You just built the foundation. Every rep counted — now recover well and come back ready.

good: Hips rise to a straight diagonal line from knee to shoulder
Feel the glutes contracting hard at the top — not the lower back taking over
bad: Overextending the lumbar spine to gain extra height

good: Lower back stays completely pinned to the floor as limbs extend
Feel the deep abdominals bracing to hold the spine still
bad: Allowing the lower back to arch away from the floor — reduce range if needed

good: Band stretches to chest width with shoulder blades squeezing together
Feel the mid-back muscles initiating the pull — not the hands or arms
bad: Shrugging the shoulders toward the ears during the movement

good: Chest stays tall, elbows track inside the knees, weight through the whole foot
Feel the floor pressing back up through both feet as you stand — drive the knees out
bad: Collapsing the knees inward on the ascent

good: Dumbbells glide close to the legs, spine stays long from head to tailbone
Feel a deep stretch accumulating in the hamstrings as you hinge — not the lower back
bad: Rounding the upper or lower back during the descent

good: Dumbbells move in a slight arc path, elbows at roughly 45° from the torso
Feel the chest muscles doing the pressing — imagine squeezing the dumbbells toward each other at the top
bad: Flaring the elbows out perpendicular to the torso

good: Torso stays vertical throughout, elbows drive straight back toward the hips
Feel the shoulder blades retracting before the arms finish pulling
bad: Leaning back aggressively to complete the rep

good: Arms lift to shoulder height with a slight forward lean and thumbs angled slightly down
Feel the side of the shoulder doing all the work through the full range
bad: Shrugging or swinging the dumbbells — if momentum is needed, the weight is too heavy

good: Body forms a perfectly straight line from head to heels
Brace hard as if bracing for an impact to the stomach — breathe steadily throughout
bad: Holding the breath during the hold

days.focusTag: Run + Conditioning — Aerobic Base & Metabolic Work
days.completionMessage: Aerobic base built, conditioning logged. Your half marathon starts here — one session at a time.

good: A pace at which you can hold a full conversation — slow and controlled
Breathing should feel rhythmic and manageable — never laboured or gasping
bad: Running too fast in early weeks — aerobic base must be built before intensity is added

good: The bell floats to shoulder height driven purely by a sharp, powerful hip snap
Feel the glutes firing like a punch at the top of every rep
bad: Squatting the movement — the knees should not track forward; this is a hip hinge

good: Full body extends tall overhead, then aggressively drives the ball into the floor
Generate force from the lats and core — the arms are just conduits for the power
bad: Using only arm strength — if the hips and core aren't involved, reset the pattern

good: Drive through the heel of the stepping leg — stand completely tall at the top
Feel the lead glute doing all the work on the way up — the back foot is just balance
bad: Pushing off with the trailing foot — load must stay on the working leg

good: Body moves as one rigid unit — no hip break as the arms extend forward overhead
Feel the deep core bracing hard to resist the extension force throughout
bad: Letting the hips sag toward the floor — keep a plank position throughout

good: Hips are stacked and elevated — a straight lateral line from head to feet
Feel the obliques working to hold the hips up
bad: Letting the hips drop toward the floor

days.focusTag: Full Body Strength B — Posterior Chain & Accessory
days.completionMessage: Three days complete. That's a full week of work in the bank — rest, eat well, and repeat.

good: Torso upright, both hips working into internal and external rotation simultaneously
Feel a deep release in the hip capsule — breathe slowly into the tightest point
bad: Forcing the range aggressively — let the hip open with each exhale

good: Smooth, fluid spinal articulation from the tailbone to the crown of the head
Feel each vertebra moving individually — not just the two extremes
bad: Rushing — the value is in full sequential spinal mobility through the whole range

good: Torso stays upright, front knee tracks over the second toe throughout the descent
Feel the front glute and quad doing all the work — weight pressed through the front heel
bad: Letting the front knee cave inward or the torso fold forward under load

good: Hips drive to full extension at the top — body forms a flat horizontal table
Feel maximum glute contraction at lockout — squeeze hard before lowering
bad: Hyperextending the lower back at the top — the movement ends at hip level, not beyond

good: Elbow drives straight back, body stays completely still — no rotation
Feel the lat pulling the elbow toward the hip — not the bicep doing a curl
bad: Rotating the torso to complete the rep — that means the weight is too heavy

good: Dumbbells travel in a vertical path, core braced, ribs stay down throughout
Feel the shoulders doing the pressing — the trunk is a stable base, not a lever
bad: Arching the lower back — brace the core and actively pull the ribs down before pressing

good: Hands pull to face level, elbows flare high — mimicking a double bicep pose at the top
Feel the rear deltoids and external rotators doing the work — not the biceps
bad: Pulling too low or letting the elbows drop — height of the elbows is everything here

good: Arms fully extend while the band tries to rotate the body — resist that rotation completely
Feel the anti-rotation tension through the obliques and deep core
bad: Rotating toward or away from the anchor at any point — stay completely square

good: Lower back stays flat to the floor as opposite limbs extend slowly overhead and out
Feel the deep stabilisers bracing — belly gently drawn inward before each rep
bad: Arching the lower back away from the floor — reduce the range of motion if this occurs

cards.title: Half Marathon Build
cards.body: Your run on Day 2 is the foundation of your half marathon journey. For the first 4 weeks, keep all runs at Zone 2 — a pace where you can hold a full conversation. From week 5, begin adding 5 minutes per week to your run duration. Do not add pace and distance at the same time. If run-walk intervals are needed in early weeks, use a 3-min run / 1-min walk structure and progress from there.

cards.title: Recovery Is Training
cards.body: Your stress and sleep scores mean recovery is currently your biggest limiter — not effort in the gym. Prioritise 7–8 hours of sleep and consider a 10-minute wind-down routine before bed. On high-stress days, reduce your effort level by one notch rather than skipping the session entirely.

cards.title: Nutrition for Recomposition
cards.body: With goals of muscle gain and fat loss simultaneously, aim for a moderate protein intake of 1.6–2.0g per kg of bodyweight daily — roughly 110–136g for you. Keep calories at or slightly below maintenance. Avoid aggressive restriction, which will impair both training performance and muscle retention. Prioritise protein at every meal.

cards.title: Progressive Overload
cards.body: Add weight only when you can complete all sets and reps with perfect technique at the prescribed effort level. A small increase of 1–2kg per exercise per week is ideal at this stage. Never sacrifice tempo or form to lift heavier. Track your weights each session — progress is the goal, not just effort.

cards.title: Your First Goal Is Technique, Not Load
cards.body: In every session, the weight you use is secondary to how the rep looks and feels. If the tempo breaks down, if you feel your lower back on the RDL, or if your knees cave in the squat — that is your signal to reduce load immediately. Novice lifters who chase weight too early plateau faster and accumulate movement faults that take months to undo.

cards.title: Track Your Numbers From Session One
cards.body: Write down every weight you use, every set, and every rep in this cycle. A notes page on your phone is enough. By the end of Week 5, looking back at Week 1's weights will show you exactly how far you've already come — and that evidence matters for long-term motivation more than almost anything else.
